desc:
The road ends here outside the high, arched gateway that, if entered, would lead
you through the walls and into the old city of Fornost. In ancient times this
was the seat of mighty human warlords until it was besieged and taken by the
Witch-king of Angmar. Although retaken shortly after, the city lay in ruins
after the fierce battles. It remained in that state for many centuries to come,
and only recently has it found new inhabitants. The Dunedain have started the
slow process of restoring the city to its former importance and glory.
name: Before the Gates of Fornost

desc:
The road here is flat, and free from holes and cracks. The land surrounding it
is trimmed and pruned, and the tall gates of Fornost dominate the land to the
north. A trail into a dark forest breaks off from the road and turns east,
towards the wooded hills in the distance.
name: Road to Fornost

desc:
A small hill to the north guides the Road east, and far to the west, a simple
wooden house sits among a sea of well-manicured grass. The road here is well
maintained, and the cracks between each stone are free of weeds and grass. The
road continues far to the south.
name: Road to Fornost

desc:
This solitary hill rises high above the farmlands to the southwest, and the Road
to Fornost to the east. A single mature pine tree provides a wide swath of shade
on the rocky ground, and a home for squirrels and birds. The ground beneath the
tree is littered with pine cones and needs.
name: Stony Hill near Fornost

desc:
A handful of tall oaks stand on the uneven ground here. Aged, broken branches
lie amidst a heavy covering of bushes and vines. The rocky ground gradually
starts rising to the east, leading to the first foothills of the Northern Downs.
name: Clump of Trees in Heavy Brush

desc:
The stony road here is swept free of detritus, and continues far to the north
and south. A small, less trodden path splits away from the road to the west,
past a small farm house, and then towards open plains. A dense forest creeps up
to the Road to the east, and little can be seen of its dark interior.
name: Fork in the Road to Fornost

desc:
An uneven dirt path meanders east-west here amongst a grassy field. Pebbles and
larger rocks have collected in its numerous depressions. Stout grass and short
weeds grow in large ruts that run on each side of the path. The path widens to
the east, as it connects to a larger road.
name: Eastern End of a Dirt Path

desc:
The dirt path here is mostly even and free from dips and ruts. Low, neatly
stacked stone walls line the path on each side. A faint walkway meanders through
skinny gaps in the northern and southern walls, allowing access to the farmland
beyond.
name: Dirt Path near a Farmhouse

desc:
A dirt path runs east-west here amongst the vast farmland. The path has a few
bumps and dips, but is free from large rocks and flora. A thick collection of
wheat grows right off the path to the north. The wheat on the southern side is
sparse and seemingly randomly placed.
name: Dirt Path near Wheat Fields

desc:
A well-managed dirt path is bordered on the west by a rocky cliff. A large pile
of haphazardly strewn boulders and gnarled branches lies directly under it. A
vast grassy field opens to the south. The path extends eastward through the
manicured farmland.
name: Eastern End of a Dirt Path

desc:
A white gazebo sits in a manicured grassy field. Tapered white spires support a
peaked roof, and within it a white marble table is surrounded by decorated
chairs of pine. A cluster of mature oak trees surrounds it, and aged,
decomposing leaves adorn its roof. A white wooden fence borders the field to the
south.
name: Grassy Field near a Farm

desc:
Tiny clumps of grass struggle to grow in this dusty, arid field. The ground is
dimpled with divets, and roots poke in and out of the earth from a small cluster
of oak trees. The mostly featureless landscape continues in all directions but
east, where a rock-filled ditch prevents further passage.
name: Dusty Field

desc:
The smell of dirt, hay, and manure permeates these long, squat, animal stables.
Twin rows of large pens provide homes to horses, mules, and cows. Wet hay
surrounds water buckets placed in each stall. Black mouse droppings create
meandering paths amongst the straw.
name: Farm Stables

desc:
Potatoes, onions, and carrots grow here in straight rows. Chickens and mice peck
at the vegetables as they scurry amongst the field. Flies and maggots feast on
fallen carrots. A long wooden fence to the south indicates the end of the farm.
Three scarecrows, in the shape of orc warriors, stand guard amidst the
vegetation.
name: Vegetable Field

desc:
Pumpkins, beans, and onions grow here in neatly managed rows. Depressions
between the rows run east-west through the field. A low stone fence surrounds
this field to the west and north. A black cloud of flies surrounds a large heap
of compost that decomposes next to a wooden fence to the south. Two scarecrows
in the shape of mountain trolls keep watchful eyes on the field.
name: Vegetable Field

desc:
Yellow stalks of mature barley cover this vast, flat field. No path is
discernible through the field, but the root-free ground provides easy access
through the stalks. To the east, the faint outline of a red house can be seen,
and tall cliffs border the field to the west and north.
name: Barley Field

desc:
A circular brick fireplace serves as the centrepiece to this richly adorned
farmhouse. A thick grizzly bear pelt surrounds it, and above it, a long, brick
chimney rises high to the ceiling. A sturdy bed made from oak beams, and adorned
with black bear pelts and cotton sheets, sits near the fireplace. Large windows
in each wall overlook the surrounding wheat fields and forests.
name: Farmhouse

desc:
An old bench, grass and weeds form the entirety of the interior of this decaying
wooden structure. The walls are made of old oak beams, and the simple gable roof
is patchy, providing minimal protection from the elements. Vines have wrapped
their leafy arms around the wood, and insects crawl among the plant. The
ramparts of Fornost can be seen from the eastern entrance.
name: Decaying Waystation near the Gates of Fornost

desc:
The grassy ground here is marked by large quartz rocks and small oak trees. The
barest outline of a path runs westward and meanders around tiny gulleys and the
occasional mature pine. The Fornost walls can be seen to the distant northeast,
and the plains continue far to the east and west.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
The flat ground here is covered with a carpet of short grass, and dotted with
large quartz rocks, and juvenile oak trees. Far to the east, the land rises to
form a small hill. To the northeast, the stony ramparts of Fornost stand
watchful guard.
name: Reedy Plains near a Hill

desc:
Long, bare trunks of oak and pine are stacked in a large pile here. Insects
scurry about the dark and protected recesses of its interior. The bottom half of
a long tube forms the mouth of a small lumber mill. Surrounding it, wood
shavings and dust have created a brown frosting on the ground. Rows of finely
cut wood planks are neatly stacked to the side of the mill.
name: Lumber Mill in a Rocky Field

desc:
The ground here is soft, with a light coating of pointed leaves and short grass.
A vast clustering of mature oak trees provides shade to weary travellers, and
nesting spots for birds and insects. Small holes dot the landscape, their dark
interiors hidden from all but the smallest animals. A tiny stream meanders
through a snaking depression in the forest floor.
name: Oak Forest

desc:
Young oak trees compete for sunlight across the uneven ground. The canopy is
thin here, providing little protection from the wind and rain. The trees are
coloured various shades of grey; from off-white to near black. Large, tumbled
boulders lie in a giant heap at the western end of this young forest. A small
stream flows from the east here, and disappears into the crevices of the
boulders to the west.
name: Oak Forest under Rocky Peaks

desc:
Tall wheat shoots cover the flat landscape here. The thick canopy of wheat
obscures any path that may run through it. The field is contained by towering
cliffs to the north and east, and a large pile of boulders to the south. The
rock pile is topped by twisted sticks and decomposing leaves.
name: Wheat Field near Towering Cliffs

desc:
Rippled columns of green wheat cover the flat landscape, completely hiding the
root-covered ground beneath it. The thick collection of grain sways to the whims
of the wind. A small rocky cliff rises far to the north, obscuring the land
beyond it.
name: Wheat Field Basin

desc:
Thick bushes and tall grass indicate light usage of the trail here. To the north
and south, the brush is thick and foreboding, but a glimpse of the Road to
Fornost indicates that the edge of the undergrowth isn't very far to the west.
name: Beaten Trail

desc:
The rocky, pockmarked, and mossy walls of Fornost dominate the western horizon.
Ramparts, filled with tall slits, top the fortified walls. Tall vegetation is
very sparse here, and few places exist that do not have a direct line of sight
to the watching eyes of the ramparts. A small stream runs east-west here,
disappearing under the fortified walls of Fornost.
name: Outside the Walls of Fornost

desc:
In the centre of the field, a single, lonely oak tree stands testament to the
slow passage of growth and decay that surrounds it. Bulbous and pointy leaves,
in various stages of decomposition, feed the ground under it. The land rises
sharply to the north, into a tall mountain range. A stream of clear water runs
through a small valley, near the southern edge of the plains. A clump of dark
thorns grows to the east.
name: Reedy Plains near Fornost

desc:
A deep lake of clear water is framed on the east and west by tall, granite
crags. Very little direct sunlight reaches the ground here, and the water is
very cold, year round. Large, oblong, granite slabs form giant mounds on the
eastern and western edges of the lake. A large hole in the southern cliff can be
seen, high above the water line.
name: Flooded Gorge between Granite Cliffs

desc:
A simple, caved-in wooden shack sits amongst overturned wooden buckets, and
skeletonised animal carcasses. Dark water has pooled inside the shack, and the
surrounding ground is undisturbed. A long, ratty string is strung between two
tall pines, and next to it, leaves collect at the base of a large hole.
name: Old Camp in a Rocky Gorge

desc:
The land here holds more vegetation than what seems possible, with bushes, and
even some smaller, scrawny trees growing on what limited soil they can find.
This gorge starts from a steep slope to the north and runs southward, through
twin pillars of granite cliffs.
name: Lightly Wooded Gorge

desc:
Grey and white boulders, of all shapes and sizes, cover the rocky hilltop here,
providing small shelter from the winds. To the east, the land slopes downward
into a lightly wooded gorge. Further east, sharp, grey hills tower over the land
like rows of giant teeth.
name: Cragged Hilltop in the Northern Downs

desc:
The top of this hill is mostly flat and lacks prominent features aside from a
few boulders and a single green shrub. This hill ends abruptly with a
precipitous cliff to the west, revealing a deep, narrow, and brush-filled
crevice and a wooded valley beyond it. The earth slopes downward under the roots
of the shrub.
name: Barren Hill above a Crevice

desc:
The seeping granite walls are carved flat here, and the ground is covered in
leveled, slick rock. The northern wall slants upward at a steep angle,
preventing travel up it. There is no ceiling here, only an open, dark expanse.
Dark driblets of water seep down the wall, forming a tiny pool at the floor.
name: Humid Passageway

desc:
The southeast edge of this hilltop is quite steep, and offers a nice view of the
flooded gorge to the east. The gorge is contained by rock walls on all sides but
its north. The hills to the north meet several tall rock faces, set apart like a
row of stairs meant for giant feet. A small staircase is carved into the
southwest edge of this hill, offering a safe passage to the plains below. A
small stream runs south of this hill, fed by a wide crack in the cliff-face to
the east.
name: Precipitous Hilltop

desc:
The eastern cliffs allow only midday lighting of this wooded and beaten path.
The brush is thin here, but even so, the path disappears quickly as it leads
further into the darkness to the west and south. A large oak tree takes its
final rest next the path here, as its wood slowly becomes part of the earth.
name: Beaten Trail in the Brush

desc:
Grass grows in small patches between large rocks on this grey hill. A winding
trail in the valley below dips in and out of sight, through the thin canopy of
treetops and underbrush. Far to the north, the bulbous mounds of the Northern
Downs seem stacked on top of one another, an undulating mass of rock and dirt.
name: Grey Hills above a Winding Trail

desc:
The hilltop here is mostly flat, and a light path amidst the dirt runs eastward,
but is quickly obscured amongst the rocky landscape. Tiny plants struggle to
grow in the inhospitable ground here. Twin crags far to the north stand high
over flat plains of mixed green, grey, and brown.
name: Barren Hills above a Lush Basin

desc:
Large slabs of angled, sheer rock create mini valleys and hilltops on top of
this grey hill. To the south and west, the hill drops into a precipitous slide
to the basin floor, far below. A long stretch of verdant brush extends far to
the west. The rippled walls of Fornost can be seen very far to the northwest.
name: Steep, Rocky Hill

desc:
Well-manicured grass stretches for miles across the green-grey plains. Far to
the south, lush hills rise from the flat ground, dotting the horizon. The flat,
green plains continue for miles to the east, and a tall cliff of granite rises
to the north.
name: Green Plains near Tall Hills

desc:
Swaths of tree stumps across the hilly plains provide a feeding ground for
insects and white fungi. A myriad of small birds are perched on the stumps,
pecking at the insects. Tall weeds, thorny vines, and seeding grass all compete
for sunlight between the stumps. A small pool of clear water sits in the middle
of the field. Two arms of flowing water sprout from it; one feeding, and the
other draining.
name: Deforested Land

desc:
Aged pines grow sporadically here, forming a sparse forest. The thin canopy of
treetops does little to dampen rainfall, or light from the sun or stars. The
uneven terrain here is marked by thick root networks and large boulders. The
rocky land rises to the north, as the forest continues.
name: Aged Pine Forest

desc:
Tall pine trees have created a carpet of soft needles, amongst the rocks and
roots in this thin forest. Clumps of white and blue fungi sprout from fallen
trunks, and their cracked skin provides home to colonies of termites and ants.
The land slopes downward to the south, into a large basin.
name: Thin Pine Forest

desc:
Small groups of juvenile pines and oak grow on this narrow hilltop. Colonies of
ants run up and down their trunks, bringing food to their homes in the rocky
ground. The rolling hills continue to the north and south, and a sheer
cliff-face lies far to the west. The top of a sparse forest lies to the east,
and the ramparts of Fornost rise above it.
name: Narrow Peak above a Forest

desc:
Tall grass grows in clumps here, amid dirt patches that dot the rocky basin. A
few large boulders, intermixed with decomposing tree trunks, lie close to the
western cliff-face. The vast basin continues far to the north and south, and the
cliff-face extends far to the north and south.
name: Grassy Patches between Tall Hills

desc:
Short shrubs, rock, and aged roots dot the landscape here. Large boulders, some
embedded deep into the ground, decorate the field. A sheer rock face rises high
to the west. Far to the east the barren land almost disappears completely, as it
forms deep valleys between the tall hills.
name: Shallow Valley amongst Tall Hills

desc:
A sea of tall grass is framed to the south and west by tall, rocky cliffs. A
cluster of mature beech and oak trees form a dense canopy near the western
cliff. Steps on the western face provide nesting spots for birds, and perching
spots for short and gnarled trees. High upon the southern rock face, an overhang
forms a small ledge. Under it, a shadowy crack snakes its way to the ground.
name: Verdant Basin under Grey Cliffs

desc:
The ground on this hilltop is mostly flat, and laden with an assortment of
weeds, faded oak leaves, and budding trees. Steep drop-offs to the east and
south provide wide vistas of the surrounding land. To the south, a jumbled mix
of oak trees form a patchwork of white, grey, and black. The grey ramparts of
Fornost stand guard to the east. Below the southern end of the cliff, a ripple
of water flows from the east, and into the forest to the south.
name: Wide Peak above an Oak Forest

desc:
A sticky substance covers the walls and floor of this small tunnel, making
travel difficult. Large webs hang from the ceiling, and a thick syrup of a white
slime drips from it. Very little outside light reaches this rocky hole, and
farther east, the light becomes rarer still. A crack runs the length of wall to
the north.
name: Tunnel of Sticky Webs

desc:
A strange web-like substance is clinging to all surfaces of the tunnel here,
giving the atmosphere a heavy, muffled feeling. Strange sounds come from a
large, web-filled hole in the cave floor. A small breeze from the west ruffles
the webs.
name: Sticky Tunnel

desc:
Short pine trees are scattered haphazardly across this rocky clifftop. A sharp
drop-off to the east gives way to a vast colouring of green, as a pine forest
fills the valley below. The tall ramparts of Fornost peek above the treetops,
far to the southeast.
name: Clifftop over a Pine Forest

desc:
The long shadows created by the surrounding hills have allowed only the most
hardy plants to thrive. Toppled oak trees, lined with white fungal crowns, lie
amidst a field of seedy grass. High on the western cliff, a single gnarled tree
provides a resting stop for migratory birds.
name: Tall Grass between Mountains

desc:
The ground here is mostly flat and rocky, and slopes downward to the south. A
few fallen oak trees provide a home for colonies of carpenter ants, and a food
source for a multicoloured fungus. High on the western cliff, a long, rocky
overhang casts the ground below it in perpetual shadow.
name: Rocky Field between Crags

desc:
This rocky basin is scattered with small shrubs, and patches of tall grass. A
sheer cliff-face towers over the basin to the west. On it, two mangled trees
live, intertwined high on a small ledge. Their branches reach eastward, toward
sunlight.
name: Pebbly Field amongst Rolling Hills

desc:
The ground here is slippery with a covering of small pebbles, and among them
lies the broken remnants of a wooden watchtower. Green fungi and red moss feed
on the aged wood. Rolling hills continue far to the north and south. To the
east, the hilltop drops precipitously, and farther east still, a small line of
hilltops sits among a deep basin.
name: Pebbly Clifftop

desc:
Thick blades of crabgrass form a spiky carpet on this hilltop. Cracks in the
stony ground create small crevices, and short shrubs reach for sunlight within
them. A vast grassy basin lies to the east, and a sheer cliff-face towers over a
shallow basin to the west.
name: Lush Hill in Verdant Plains

desc:
A decay-laden husk of an abandoned house stands here, sunken deep into the
grassy basin. A dead oak tree is resting across the heavily-bent bow of the
house. Three leafy vines run the length of the tree, providing a feeding ground
for butterflies and birds.
name: Grass-covered Ruins

desc:
A vast sea of green ends abruptly at granite cliffs to the west and south. The
uneven land is decorated by clusters of tall oak and pine trees. Tall granite
cliffs edge the grassy plain to the south and west, their featureless faces
standing testament to the growth and decay of the basin.
name: Green Field near Plunging Cliffs

desc:
A clump of pine trees creates a mini forest at the peak of this grassy hill.
Pinecones litter the ground around it, and the shadowy recesses of massive
boulders create a resting ground for large animals. A vast basin of green and
grey lies to the north.
name: Northern Hills

desc:
Precipitous drops frame this hilltop in all directions but north. To the south
and southwest, a vast forest of green, speckled with notes of brown, colours the
valley below. To the southeast, the ramparts of Fornost hover above the horizon.
name: Hilltop above a Pine Forest

desc:
Large planks of rotten, moss-covered wood lean against a giant slab of granite,
here in a grassy basin. Two large wooden posts stand as sentinels in the field,
slowly decomposing, and feeding the vegetation around it. Tall hills rise far to
the east and south.
name: Decomposing Ruins in a Grassy Basin

desc:
The grassy, reedy field extends far to the north, before it is abruptly
interrupted by a towering cliff of grey stone. The land here slopes down to the
south and east, and tips of a green forest stab the horizon to the southeast.
Two mature pine trees grow next to each other, forming a tiny forest and animal
habitat.
name: Field in the Northern Downs

desc:
The ground slants downhill here, as the valley narrows a bit to the south. The
tall hills that surround this valley cast it in shadow for most of the day,
allowing only the hardiest of flora to survive. Large granite slabs dot the
landscape, and few tall trees grow from the rocky ground. The valley continues
in all directions.
name: Stony Valley between Grey Cliffs

desc:
The soft and spongy ground here is pockmarked with small, foot- and hoof-sized
depressions. A large boulder is here, adorned with red moss, and a tuft of grass
grows on its top. Blue irises, arrowheads, and brooklime grow abundantly across
the wet terrain.
name: Marsh in the Northern Downs

desc:
The valley narrows here, as the tall crags push inwards from the east and west.
The forest floor is littered with oblong slabs of rock, and the carcasses of
long-dead trees. The vegetation thickens a bit to the south, as the widening
valley allows a greater cover of sunlight.
name: Lightly Forested Valley

desc:
The tips of pine trees form soft spikes upon the horizon to the east. The forest
sways in unison as gusts of wind arrive, driven by the mountains to the east.
The tall crag to the west blocks all sunlight but the morning rays, causing the
vegetation here to be short, and sparse. The uneven terrain drops sharply to the
east, and the field extends far to the north.
name: Shadowy Field amongst Tall Crags

desc:
The land slopes gently to the west, but ends abruptly to the east and south, as
it plunges rapidly to the grassy valley below. A seemingly random assortment of
partially built wood structures dots the green-grey basin to the west.
name: Hills above a Ruined Town

desc:
Thin wisps of grass struggle to grow in the rocky ground here. Large boulders
lie along the cliff's edge, as if placed intentionally. A long series of steps
is carved into the eastern cliff, leading to the marshy ground below. The
Northern Downs fill the horizon in all directions. Far under a sudden drop-off
to the east, a tiny dot of water ripples.
name: Cliff above a Natural Spring

desc:
Two mature oak trees provide support to the leaning walls of a broken house. A
decayed hole in the centre of the rotting floorboards provides nourishment to a
young oak tree. Disintegrating wood planks poke through the leafy ground,
creating a jagged, shattered memory of a home.
name: Aged Ruins in a Lush Basin

desc:
Heaps of rotting wood is hidden in the depths of tall grass and weeds in this
overgrown field. A stout post stands watch in the field, and the four small
holes in it provide a home for insects, and a feeding spot for small birds. A
rocky cliff rises deep within a hill to the west.
name: Ruins near a Rocky Cliff

desc:
This peak sits high above a range of rolling hilltops that lead far south. A
vast grassy basin continues far to the east, where it meets a small crag range.
Far to the west, a massive, grey rock face towers over a basin of stone and
grass.
name: Rocky Hilltop above a Grassy Basin

desc:
The crags of the Northern Downs stretch northward, eventually meeting the first
hills of the Rammas Formen. The western cliff has heavily eroded, and opens into
a narrow valley that snakes southward. To the east, the land drops quickly,
ending in a small field. To the south, the mountains slowly slope downward,
revealing the gentler plains beyond.
name: Northern Downs

desc:
The curved cliff-face protects this area from the harsh winds, rain, and snow
that occur in the Northern Downs. An abandoned fire pit, covered in twigs and
brown leaves, sits in a small depression near the northern cliff. A thick, ratty
rope is suspended from two mature oak trees. Strips of grey and brown hemp hang
from it, still from the lack of breeze.
name: Old Campsite under Towering Peaks

desc:
A long stretch of grassy plateau ends with a cleaved drop-off to the south. A
lonely maple sapling grows at the apex of this hill, peeking above the top of
the tall grass. The land slowly slopes upward to the east and north, as the
rolling hills continue.
name: Grass in the Northern Downs

desc:
A steep ridge among the mountains connects the two adjacent plateaus, and offers
a safer way to travel than climbing the precipitous and jagged crags. A mossy
wall of rock towers ominously over the slope to the north, like a giant wave in
a sea of grey stone.
name: Ridge in the Northern Downs

desc:
The rocky hill ends with a steep slope to the south, providing a wide vista of a
light forest. The unbroken land below continues far to the south, climbing
slowly downward, towards the flat plains of Fornost. Far to the east, a giant
mound of loose rock, and detritus, is set against a tall, grey face of stone.
name: Craggy Hill above a Light Forest

desc:
This sharp corner of a rocky plateau sits high above the area surrounding the
city of Fornost, providing a wide view over the hills. A single gnarled tree
grows here, its height stunted, and body twisted by the rocky ground. A dark
forest covers the landscape far to the south, and little can be seen of its
interior from this distance.
name: Thin Clifftop in the Northern Downs

desc:
Aside from some boulders and a few bushes, there is only a flat plateau here.
The rocky plateau is bounded by a steep cliff to the west, and a narrow crevice
to the north. There is a brush-filled valley beyond the western cliff, hinting
that these stair-like crags of the Northern Downs are coming to an end.
name: Rugged Plateau in the Northern Downs

desc:
The western edge of this rocky hill slopes sharply downward, and a vast expanse
of green-grey hills opens below it. Two large boulders sit side-by-side to the
north, and a third, smaller rock, sits wedged between them. Two large mossy
patches, coloured green and red, adorn the southern faces of the boulders.
name: Edge of a Pebbly Cliff

desc:
Thick brushwood fills the western entrance to this narrow crevice. Steep and
jagged cliffs surround the crevice from all sides, making this place a natural
dead end. To the east, the crevice gets even narrower and slopes downward into
the rock.
name: Narrow Crevice

desc:
Shifting slices of sky reveal themselves through the thick canopy in this old
forest. The root-covered land slopes downward to the south; and farther south
still, the top of Fornost's stone walls hovers above the treetops. A small
stream meanders through a deep depression in the soil.
name: Forested Valley

desc:
Layered slabs of sheer granite form giant, rolling steps, across this rocky
hill. Vegetation here is very sparse, and only a few patches of moss provide a
hairy texture to the sheer rock. The vast, grey expanse of Fornost lies below to
the south. Tall crags and rolling hills dot the landscape, east to west, across
the northern expanse.
name: Isolated Rocky Hill

desc:
The well-maintained road here continues far to the north and south. The
expertly-maintained road travels with no wind or deviation, and a thick brush to
the east grows close to the road's edge. The brush grows so closely together
that it conceals its interior well from sun and starlight. In contrast, a deep,
rock-filled ditch to the west prevents passage to vast and airy land.
name: Road to Fornost

desc:
A closed depression drains underground here, forming a cylindrical tubelike
cavern. Only several yards in dimension, there is little room to move about.
Large tree roots protrude from the karst walls, giving off the appearance of
fingers reaching out at those who dare enter. A smooth limestone chute descends
downwards, while a break in the ceiling provides an exit up.
name: Karst Doline

desc:
A mammoth spherical hollow has been etched out by aeons of water erosion. The
ashen-grey limestone walls glisten with abundant moisture that seeps through
endlessly. The central space of this large cavern room is clean from any fallout
or debris, yet huge stalagmites and stalactites form in staggering numbers as
the cavern's floor and ceiling meet.
name: Central Dome

desc:
This portion of the cave is of moderate dimensions but richly decorated by
numerous cave formations. However, it is hard to focus on anything else other
than the foetid odour that saturates the air and assaults the nose from the
massive piles of guano that have accumulated over countless years. The grotto
opens into a large dome to the south, and a large tree-like column formation can
be seen to the north.
name: Dank Grotto

desc:
It appears at one time the cave system continued onwards in multiple directions
but is now blocked by a massive pile of rubble. Boulders of innumerable size and
shape form a makeshift wall to the north and west. A stalagmite and stalactite
have fused together to form a single massive column. The column gives off the
appearance of a branchless tree trunk, gnarled and cracked by countless years
passed. The cavern passage continues to slope down from the east and descends
further south.
name: Rubble-filled Passage

desc:
A rotted cesspool has formed here in a cavern sump, the drainage focal point for
the cave system. Murky swirls of red intermingle with masses of algae and other
indistinguishable detritus. A heavy inescapable odour lingers in the stale air.
The occasional drip can be heard, as condensation and run-off collects and falls
from the cavern ceiling.
name: At a Cesspool

desc:
A massive outcrop of gypsum flowers has formed along the walls of the cave,
reminiscent of a bed of spring ephemerals. Delicate and precarious, the
bone-white petals of the crystal blossoms spiral out in innumerable shapes and
sizes. Sizeable amounts of cave fallout lie scattered on the cave floor, making
any quick movement increasingly difficult.
name: Cavern Tunnel

desc:
No trace of daylight ever reaches this part of the cave. Sloping downwards from
the bear den to the north, several enormous flowstones and other cave formations
begin to appear in growing numbers. Despite the lack of light, a small breeze
can still be felt blowing in from the north. A natural stair of limestone
descends deeper into the cave system while another passage breaks off to the
south.
name: Sloping Tube

desc:
A huge flowstone formation sits in the centre of this cavern room, and takes on
the form of what appears to be a bear in a deep, tranquil sleep. Adjacent to
formation is a small cavern pool that sits perfectly still, mirroring the
formation and cavern ceiling. To the north a natural passage can be seen snaking
both upwards and to the west.
name: Odd Formation

desc:
Twin column formations oppose each other here in this terminal portion of the
cave. Albeit thin, the columns appear stately in nature while giving off an odd
appearance of symmetry. Above large amounts of ceiling straws and drapery loom
overhead, while several fallen pieces lie shattered on the cave floor. Moving
north and west are the only options as the cave dead ends.
name: At Twin Columns

desc:
Here an omnipresent silence permeates throughout the deepest recess of the
caverns. There is no trace of any cave stream or even drips of water from the
surrounding walls. The air is dry and fettered, and the strong stench of decay
wafts in from the north and east. Nary any cave formations are found here given
the absence of waterflow.
name: Dead Cave

desc:
The cave system here is mostly likely home to some species of bear as large
chunks of fur litter the cavern floor. A few piles of crushed, dried leaves can
be seen nestled next to the eastern cave wall. The cave entrance can still be
seen to the north, while to the south a darkening abyss grows.
name: Bear Den

desc:
Jagged outcrops of limestone rock jut out from a large sinkhole, poking and
prodding in every direction. Patches of animal fur can be see littered amongst
the rubble, torn on the rocky outcroppings. Tangled vines and crooked trees edge
the cave mouth above, precariously growing from its serrated maw.
name: Sinkhole

desc:
A disturbingly massive mound of animal skeletons lies here, acting as a
make-shift tomb for countless species of animal prey. The skeletons rot in
various states of decomposition, crushed and discarded in reckless abandon. It
appears some poor soul met their untimely demise here as humanoid remains can be
seen amongst the carcases.
name: Dead End

desc:
The trail here is obscured in parts by the encroachment of surrounding trees,
and the cover of thick brush. Meandering roots create shelter for insects and
tiny animals, and a feeding ground for snakes and honey badgers. Little sunlight
penetrates the ground below the undergrowth.
name: Meandering Trail through Thick Vegetation

desc:
Towering cliffs of grey and brown hedge the brush-laden path to the east and
north. On the battered path, shattered pieces of white stone lay embedded in the
rocky soil. The path turns west and south here, but the thick undergrowth
quickly overtakes it, swallowing it within its depths.
name: Beaten Path near Tall Cliffs

desc:
Heavy brush encroaches on this beaten trail from all sides. The path here is
very uneven, with small, rocky bumps, slight depressions, and roots that create
wooden snares. The path disappears quickly into the thick underbrush to the east
and north. A few oak trees to the south of the path provide a wide swath of
shade.
name: Shaded Turn in a Beaten Trail

desc:
The road continues its long journey, stretching away to the north and south and
lined on both sides by trees and low bushes. Away to the west the remains of an
ancient castle wall can be seen while to the northeast the shrubs and bushes
thicken at the edge of a forbidding-looking brushland.
name: Road to Fornost

desc:
The long Road to Fornost stretches north and south as far as the eye can see.
Eastwards is a vast area of plains, which terminates in a line of hills in the
far distance. To the far west an ancient stone wall is visible through the trees
that line the road.
name: Road to Fornost

desc:
The wide road to Fornost leads north and south. The western side of the road is
lined with trees, and at this point a small area has been cleared among them to
form a small camp site for those wishing to rest from their journey. Eastwards,
a vast area of plains stretches away into the distance.
name: Road to Fornost

desc:
The road extends for miles in a perfectly straight path, reaching northwards
towards Fornost and southwards, eventually, towards Bree. To the east a large
area of plains extends for many miles, while to the west travel away from the
road is blocked by a steep rocky bank, bristling with hedges. A crumbling stone
wall can be seen on top of the bank, perhaps part of a larger fortification.
name: Road to Fornost

desc:
The road continues its long journey through the plains. To the west it is
bounded by a tall rocky outcrop, atop which can be seen some kind of old
fortifications. Eastwards, the ground becomes rough and broken suggesting travel
in that direction would be time-consuming and difficult.
name: Road to Fornost

desc:
The road curves back to resume its northward journey at this point. The road
surface is pitted and worn through use in places, though there are signs of
recent repairs, presumably the work of the Dunedain of Fornost. Not far to the
south lies a steep ravine from which the sound of running water can be heard,
while to the east lies a wooded riverbank.
name: Bend in the Road

desc:
The road bends around from south to east here, in order to avoid a large rocky
outcrop to the north atop which can be seen the crumbling walls of an ancient
castle. To the west down a steep bank can be seen a swift-running river. The
road itself is poorly-maintained this far from any settlement, showing deep
signs of wear.
name: Bend in the Road

desc:
This old and worn stone bridge spans a narrow yet deep ravine. The sound of
running water can be heard from the swift river far down below. Though old, the
stonework seems to be sound and sturdy owing to being visible reinforcement in
several key places, well able to support the traffic that passes across it.
name: Old Stone Bridge

desc:
Many trees dot the plains here south of a thick wall of thorny bush. Light heath
spreads out in all directions as far as can be seen, except for a low ridge of
hills that rises to the south-west. In the distance to the south-east is a rocky
crevice that dips steeply from the otherwise flat plains.
name: Wooded Plains

desc:
The upward slope of the plains grant a commanding view in all directions. To the
north the plains are covered in tall bushes, while to the west the plains
stretch towards hills in the distance. Eastwards the ground drops steeply into a
narrow valley, but the slope is so sharp that it is impossible to descend from
this point.
name: Rising Field

desc:
Gorse bushes and a few shrubs are the only flora to be seen on these rugged
plains. The rough terrain expands all around, except to the east where the
ground drops sharply into a narrow valley which runs north-south through the
uneven plains. The descending slope is covered with a scree of small stones such
that descent could be extremely hazardous.
name: Gorse-covered Fields

desc:
Few plants grow atop this rocky ridge, except for some short thick grass that
grows amongst the boulders. Westwards the land descends into a wide area of
plains, while to the east it dips into a narrow valley before continuing to rise
into a range of tall hills.
name: Ridge over a Valley

desc:
This flat, grassy shelf of rock juts out above the river. Below, the river
continues the process of eroding the riverbank. To the east, at the foot of a
steep bank can be seen the end of a narrow valley while below to the west can be
seen a rocky inlet. Southward a dense brush lies beyond the river and far in the
distance a narrow tower rises against the horizon.
name: Rocky Riverbank

desc:
At the edge of the brush to the north, the old trees and rough bushes of the
brushland give way to more sparsely growing plains. The ground is covered with
low undergrowth with the occasional clump of trees. Further to the south, the
greenery gives way all together.
name: Bushy Fields

desc:
The transition between the open plains to the south and the untamed brushland to
the north is marked by a dense wall of old trees. The ground is dotted with
trees and small bushes and the occasional large boulder. The trees here are much
younger, however, than those that rise amidst the bushes to the north.
name: Scrubland Border

desc:
The open plains to the south gradually give way to wiry underbrush here, and to
the north the bushes thicken greatly at the edge of a dense scrubland. The
Fornost-Bree road is not far away to the west, however, for those of a less
adventurous nature.
name: Bushy Plains

desc:
The wide plains continue unbroken, the only break from the monotony provided by
the occasional tree or gorse bush. To the west, however, the land begins to rise
gently into a range of rocky hills. Northwards, a line of trees on the horizon
marks the border of what seems to be a thick brushland.
name: Northern Plains

desc:
Here the ground begins to rise up from the plains, into a range of hills that
extend southward. The ground becomes increasingly rocky and the flora more
sparse as the hills ascend. The occasional rocky outcrop provides a good place
to pause and survey the surrounding landscape.
name: Rising Hills

desc:
This large tract of open land stretches away to the south and to the north in a
wide sea of green grass. The road is not too far away to the west, the only
visible symbol of civilisation. Far to the east stands a range of hills, among
which one tall peak stands out alone.
name: Northern Plains

desc:
The land here is flat and open, with only the occasional tree or bush to break
the monotony. The ground is covered with thick grass which seems to support a
variety of small grazing animals. Far to the east can be seen a range of hills,
but it is difficult to guess how long it would take to reach them.
name: Northern Plains

desc:
The land begins to rise gently up from the plains into a range of rocky hills
running from the north to the south. The low, bushy ridge separates broad
sections of fields to the west and flat, rocky ground to the east. The vantage
point offered here shows numerous landmarks. A road stretches out in the west,
with a large ruin beyond it.
name: Gentle Slope

desc:
The land here is wide and open, but to the west it begins to rise gently towards
a range of hills in the distance. To the south, the ground dips into a shallow
depression. North and east the plains continue as far as the eye can see.
name: Vast Northern Plains

desc:
The ground of this shallow depression is covered with thick grass and is marshy
in places, where rain-water running down from above has collected in small pools
and mires. From this low point it is not possible to discern much of the
surrounding area, except for the faint sound of running water to the south and
the top of a rocky ridge to the west.
name: Dip in the Plains

desc:
Rocks and boulders litter the ground here, as the land rises into the hills to
the north. The occasional gorse bush is the only plant-life to be seen. Here and
there little gullies have been formed by water running down the slopes after
heavy rainfall. The abundance of small stones and pebbles makes it difficult to
keep a sure footing.
name: Sparse Rocky Rise

desc:
A wide area of plains stretches away to the north here. To the east the land
dips briefly, before starting a gentle rise into a distant range of hills. To
the south, the land remains flat but becomes more uneven being covered with
rocks and boulders. The road from Bree to Fornost can be seen not far away to
the west.
name: Northern Plains

desc:
The ground here is flat though somewhat uneven, being covered with rocks and
boulders, some of great size. The occasional rustling sound indicates the
presence of some wildlife, though of what sort is unclear. The land to the north
remains flat, while to the east a small hillock rises up. To the south, a wooded
area can be seen, suggesting a water source in this otherwise desolate area.
name: Wide, Rocky Plain

desc:
This is the northern bank of a fast-flowing river. Many trees grow on the bank,
though the undergrowth is not nearly as thick as on the other side of the river,
where the edge of a dense brush can be seen. To the north, the trees give way to
rocky plains and hills. Not far from here to the west lies the Fornost-Bree
road.
name: Wooded River Bank

desc:
The wooded riverbank is bounded to the south and east by a wide river. On the
far side of the river a rising expanse of dense brushland can be seen, and only
the occasional animal cry hints as to what lies inside. North of here the sparse
trees give way to rocky hills, outriders of the North Downs which stretch all
the way to Fornost and beyond.
name: Wooded Riverside

desc:
The ground rises here to form the start of a small hillock, dotted with bushes
and low shrubs. To the north, a sharp gradient leads up into a ridge of hills,
while to the east the land flattens out to open, rocky plains. To the south the
plains gradually give way to woody plains and wild brush beyond a rushing river.
name: Sloping Plains

desc:
A natural dip in the surrounding hills and plains has formed into a little inlet
which feeds the river to the south with run-off water. The steeply rising banks
on three sides make this a secluded and tranquil spot, with only the sound of
running water disturbing the silence.
name: Rocky Inlet

desc:
The dense line of trees has been pushed back here, with scattered stumps showing
how the field was cleared to create a flat, open plot of land. Evidence of a
small camp site is still visible on the ground, a resting point off the long
road to the east that has been repeatedly used and abandoned.
name: Roughly Cleared Field

desc:
Separated by a long, waterlogged ditch, the fields run up to the road to the
east and off into the distance in all other directions. Patches of bindweed
sprout from the damp soil, covering the old marks of what used to be a trail
headed west. Rows of trees rise to the south, limiting the view of the fields
that wind between them.
name: Field by the Road

desc:
What was once a broad plot of land is now a roiling sea of spiny brush and tall
grasses, its rampant growth standing in contrast to the calmer fields to the
east and west. The bushes join a dense copse of trees to the south, where
crumbled walls peek over the horizon. The road, far to the east, is barely
visible.
name: Wildly-Growing Expanse

desc:
This broad, square patch of fallow land is abandoned and empty of crops. Far
from the safety of Fornost, the surrounding fields are much the same, left quiet
and empty to be reclaimed by the wilderness. The road is some distance to the
east, and more fields can be seen to the south.
name: Empty Plot

desc:
This field is covered in layers of weeds and other plants, growing wild despite
the aged signs that it had once been cleared for farming. Halfway to being brush
here, the farmlands vanish between dense bushes to the south but are easily
traversed to the north and east.
name: Untended Fields

desc:
This corner of the field bears the mark of tilling, but time has worn away the
work of Men. The edges of the farmland are defined by overgrown plots of trees,
the artificial borders long decayed. The empty fields stretch to the south, with
other routes barred by thickly-built fences.
name: Abandoned Farm

desc:
Trees loom all around, and thorny bushes grow in a whorl up towards the thick
branches. The only traversable path is a narrow corridor that leads north-
south, through the dense brush to the north and into more open plains to the
south. The surrounding landscape is obscured, and further landmarks cannot be
seen.
name: Among the Narrowing Thornbushes

desc:
Hidden from sight by bushes to the north and tall rows of trees that conceal the
surroundings, this field hosts hastily-arranged rows of unusual crops. A small
wooden shed holds tools and various small barrels, evidence of organised
activity in maintaining this curiously clandestine farm.
name: Tended Glade

desc:
The fallow land has given rise to wild, brambly growth, stretching from the
dense trees to the east into a broad thorny expanse to the west. The bountiful
field is hidden beneath stabbing branches and prickly thorns, giving an air of
aggression to the natural flora. The field clears out to the north and south.
name: Overgrown Field

desc:
Bushes thickly blanket the old field, their stubborn thorns sticking to all in
reach. Impassable to the west and dense to the south and east, the bushes recede
to the north. Looming grimly in the distance to the south is a great, decaying
ruin, visibly overgrown by the dense growth that surrounds it.
name: Dense Bushes

desc:
Dense thorny bushes grow tall here, sprawling across a field that is littered
with crumbled rock. To the south stands a dark mossy wall, less a construction
than an ordered pile of stones. The fields spread out to the east, with more
overgrown brush to the north.
name: Thick Bushes by a Wall

desc:
Ringed by thick bushes and dense trees, this dry and barren plot of land lies
empty of flora. A few scraggly shrubs dot the pale brown soil. Not far to the
southwest a decaying ruin creeps over the horizon, giving an altogether deathly
feel to this faded farmland. A rising hill blocks passage to the east.
name: Dry Farmland

desc:
Lying at the base of an ancient stone wall, the field is filled with scattered
stones, each weathered by time to various degrees. The decaying wall to the west
and south stands more due to its own weight than any remaining integrity in its
construction. Nearby, the sound of a river can be heard.
name: Stony Walled Field

desc:
This is a sloped patch of land ending above the northern back of a river. There
is no safe way to access the river from this vantage point, but the clear view
south shows a stone bridge to the southeast, and thick bushes reaching the old
stone fortress to the southwest. The plains continue smoothly to both the east
and west.
name: Field above a River

desc:
Saplings and low bushes grow sporadically from the thick wall of trees to the
east and south, slowly filling these plains with wild flora. The fields
themselves are uneven, showing deep signs of erosion from what was once a gentle
slope towards the plains to the east. The barely-visible road curves around to
the east and south, but there is no way to reach it from here.
name: Rough Plains

desc:
Curving around a tall slope of trees growing into the side of a hill to the
south-west, these plains are uneven and damp from runoff from the fields above.
Erosion has worn empty, depressed patches into the soil filled with stagnant
mud. More gentle elevation leads westwards, while thick bushes and a low stone
wall create a sharp border to the east.
name: Winding Fields

desc:
This small shed is strewn with hastily-stored objects. The bare wooden floor is
marked with scrapes and other marks of heavy objects being moved carelessly
across it. What those objects are is quite clear, given the stack of heavy
crates piled in a corner. The walls are well-constructed, insulating this place
from the world outside, save for a small window.
name: Small Enclosed Shed

desc:
The plains stretch out broad and flat here, west of the road. Cut off from the
farmland to the north, the open plains to the south and west leave this place
with a sense of isolation. Distantly to the south is the shadow of a great
construction, barely visible through spotty brushland and trees.
name: Northern Plains

desc:
Broad patches of trees form natural barriers to the north and south, bordering a
grassy, overgrown field. The wind faintly howls as it is channeled through this
natural corridor. The fields stretch east towards the distant road, and west
deeper into the wilderness. No other landmarks are visible.
name: Field between Trees

desc:
Enclosed by thick trees and wooden fences, this patch of field has been isolated
from the farmland barely visible around it. The soil is clear from any
underbrush, but it has not been prepared for sowing and the only signs of growth
are the slow reclamation of the surrounding wilderness.
name: Untilled Corner

desc:
The ground under the brush exists in eternal dimness, as very little light
filters through the low, bushy canopy. The thick vegetation continues far to the
east and north. Several small holes obscure dark underground passageways for
animals and insects.
name: Heavy Brush near the Road to Fornost

desc:
The brush here is very dense, and is covered in a thick layer of vines and
leaves. The ground is rooty and uneven, and small animal holes dot the ground. A
precipitous gorge opens up to the south, preventing further travel in that
direction. The heavy brush continues far to the north and east.
name: Heavy Undergrowth near a Ridge

desc:
Heavy brush continues for miles in all directions but south, where the ground
drops away into a steep cliff. The ground here has a thick layer of decomposing
leaves and moss, and this gives the brush a musty smell. No path can be gleaned
through the undergrowth.
name: Deep in Heavy Brush

desc:
A carpet of rotting leaves that covers the ground conceals damp, black soil. A
small clump of massive oaks form a small forest in the centre of the brush here.
Their thick canopy blots out all but a few sunbeams, even during the brightest
of days.
name: Forested Brush near a Winding Trail

desc:
Scattered shrubs of various shapes and sizes dot the rocky ground here. Long
growths of ivy provide a leafy covering to the bulbous flora. Several small
holes provide home to small animals, and a hunting ground for snakes and honey
badgers. The brush continues far to the east.
name: Light Vegetation near Fornost

desc:
The far end of this path is hidden beneath a dense field of wild bushes, which
abates along the trail to the south. The small path shows obvious signs of use
by some unknown creatures, leading towards the dark, mouldering castle to the
north.
name: Small Path in Some Bushes

desc:
Arched trees and thick bushes have enclosed a small hollow in the brush,
blocking sight and passage all around. A rushing stream can be heard nearby, and
thin streams of light pierce through the canopy to the ground. The path is
barely visible through the dense green thorns to the west.
name: Narrow Thorny Hollow

desc:
What was once a proud fortification has been abandoned to time and war, its
walls no longer constructions so much as heaps of crumbled stone that have yet
to topple. Earthworks are the only decor, reinforcing old stone and grasping at
the walls like great earthen claws. The dim barrow continues north, and exits
south.
name: Dark Entryway

desc:
The hallway widens slightly coming from the south, as more of the old castle's
interior is sheltered from the utter ruin of its walls. The inside, however, is
unusually cold and completely stripped of furnishings. Whatever purpose this
place once served has been lost, now serving as a tomb for forgotten Men.
name: Crumbling, Ancient Crypt

desc:
Aeons have passed since this castle hosted the living. Now, the halls only
contain hastily-built mounds of dirt and rubble, burying nameless Men from ages
past. Unusably-rusted weapons are laid beside the graves, but all broken, as if
the serviceable tools had long-since been taken. The barrow's halls lead in all
directions.
name: Darkened Barrow

desc:
The northern edge of the central crypt is in worse condition than the other
visible walls, damage from an old siege that was never repaired. Piles of
crumbled rock around the base suggest that the only reason the wall still stands
is that anything that could collapse already has. Hallways, like tunnels into
the earth, lead west and east.
name: In an Ancient Crypt

desc:
Crude stone slabs, covered in dirt and dust, raise dozens of ancient corpses off
the floor of the barrow. Even more lie buried in collapsed piles of rock,
claimed by the continued deterioration of the castle. The cold, dry air bears no
stench of the dead, their bodies having laid here for untold years.
name: An Earthen Grave

desc:
This enclosed section of the walls once would have had a specialized purpose,
but now is no more than a musty cave amidst the collapsing ruin. Spaced evenly
around the walls are ancient, dusty skeletons, heaped over partially with stone
and rusted, warped scraps. The only exit leads eastwards.
name: Circular Chamber

desc:
The toppled stairs are no longer usable, but fallen stones make for a crude
imitations as the ruins pile upwards. The old and decrepit tower seems on the
verge of collapse, its walls badly damaged by time and bearing faint evidence of
heavy impacts.
name: Base of a Decrepit Tower

desc:
The heaping stone rubble ends at the remains of what must once have been some
mighty fortification on top of the tower. The platform is still protected by
low, eroded battlements, which once would have sheltered the castle's defenders.
Despite the disrepair, this spot overlooks the surrounding countryside clearly.
To the east lies the old Road to Fornost, beyond which plains stretch away to a
line of hills in the far distance. To the southeast is the tangled Brushland of
Fornost, while to the north stands the great city itself.
name: Crumbling Battlement

desc:
A tall pile of rubble shows where an interior wall of the castle has completely
collapsed under the weight of the decaying roof, blocking off this wing of the
interior. Elements of metalwork are visible in the ruins, remnants of what once
was a gate to the east. Some of the rocks were once apparently cleared away,
perhaps for use elsewhere in the dolmen.
name: Mouldering Ruins

desc:
This room is in better condition than the rest of the ruin, showing no signs of
having been turned into a tomb. The room's size and construction suggest it was
of some importance, but there are no contents but the dust. Thin slits in the
walls provide a defensible view of the outside, and offer a source of the cold
breeze that eerily whistles through the castle's remains.
name: Bare Stone Room

desc:
Heaping rubble and dirt forms a narrow tunnel that runs to the east and ends
here, leading up towards a heavy hatch in the ceiling. The walls and floor are
built from large, heavy stone blocks, smoothed to form a hallway that now lies
littered with old, crumbled rock. The air is stagnant and quiet.
name: Beneath the Ruins

desc:
Dry, stale air hangs in this quiet stone hallway. The path is rubble-strewn and
layered with dirt, and the ancient stonework is held together more by its own
weight than its crumbled structural integrity. To the west and east is only
darkness, as this ancient stone corridor continues.
name: Empty Corridor

desc:
The roof of the tunnel has given way, collapsing to the east and permanently
sealing it. The stone walls have crumbled, and water drips slowly from the
rubble to the south. A curious explorer would have to leave to the west, where
the tunnel disappears into the darkness.
name: Rocky Dead-end

desc:
This ancient storage room, several dozen feet long and wide, contains little of
interest. The only crates, lying toppled in a corner, are metal, with all wood
presumably having rotted away. The room is cool and dry, insulated from the
environment around it despite its poor condition. Perhaps it once held
perishable goods for the castle's inhabitants.
name: Cold Cellar

desc:
Water covers the floor of this concealed basement, less than a foot deep. The
source of the water is not immediately clear, but the sounds of a river are
audible through the cracks in the eastern wall. Long years of erosion have worn
away the walls of this room, with the northern wall partially crumbled as the
stonework gives way to time.
name: Flooded Basement

desc:
Here the river curves sharply south, narrowing as it passes into a cave opening.
The current is too strong and treacherous for any person to continue safely in
that direction, however. The rocky walls of the ravine are crumbled and cracked,
worn away by centuries of erosion and tumbling rocks from the looming ruin on
the cliffs above.
name: Fast Flowing River

desc:
The path of the river bends sharply westward here, channeled by a rocky ridge to
the north and east. To the north, tall stone wall forms an artificial cliff,
reinforcing a shoreline that has been heavily eroded by runoff from the fields
above. The fast-moving water makes travel eastward more and more difficult.
name: Sharp Bend in a River

desc:
The river changes course once more from east to north at this point. The banks
to the west and south have been severely eroded by the passing waters and are
crumbling in many places. To the east, the river arrives through a narrow gap,
over which a stone bridge has been built.
name: Bend in a River

desc:
The steep banks of the river descend rapidly here, being replaced by dense
undergrowth and shrubs as the river passes into a rapidly thickening brushland.
The flora is especially dense to the southeast, from where strange noises and
animal cries can be heard. To the north, the brush thins out somewhat, giving
way to more open land beyond.
name: Swift River

desc:
The river flows swiftly westward here, hemmed in on both sides by steep banks.
Many small stones litter the riverbed and shores, and in places larger ones jut
out above the surface, making this stretch more than a little hazardous for
watercraft.
name: Swift, Narrow River

desc:
The river slows its pace somewhat here, as it widens and continues on its
journey westward. Tall bushes encroach on both the north and south banks. To the
south, the brushland becomes much thicker, and it is impossible to see more than
a short distance. To the north, the thorny bushes gradually give way to a large
area of plains beyond.
name: Broad River

desc:
The river runs more slowly here, and bends gently to the west from the north as
it meets the tall, bushy riverbank. To the south, the brush and undergrowth
remain as thick as ever, while to the north an area of rocky hills can be seen
in the distance.
name: Slow Bending River

desc:
The river bends from east to south here and increases its pace somewhat as it is
fed by water from a small inlet to the north. To the east it becomes narrower as
it passes underneath a rocky overhang. The western riverbank is sandy and
shallow, providing a convenient point to enter or leave the river.
name: Bend in a River

desc:
The river speeds up suddenly here as it passes between the eroded riverbank to
the north and a steep bank to the south. A short distance above, a rock shelf
juts out over the water, while to the east the sound of falling water can be
heard above the noise of the river itself.
name: Swift River

desc:
Tall bushes seem to grow taller as they follow the gentle rise of a mound. Just
past its peak, the crest falls away into the rapids below. While the drop is not
all that high, it is still too far to safely jump, and the eroded soil makes
climbing impossible. To the south, a sea of waving branches and dense bushes
spreads out for miles.
name: Bushy Mound

desc:
The river passes through a narrow ravine at this point, with steep walls of rock
to the north and south. The sound of the rushing water echoes from the rocks
making it impossible to hear anything else. Above, the river is spanned by an
old yet sturdy stone bridge.
name: Under a Stone Bridge

desc:
Large, twisting roots poke through the slick muddy walls in a vain attempt to
find water. The uneven floor is covered in small puddles, and glossy rocks poke
through the ground, making the tunnel a tripping hazard for unwary travellers.
The tunnel stretches to the north, and an extensive collection of roots hangs
overhead, close enough to reach.
name: Moist Tunnel

desc:
The bottom of the crack slopes downward into the rock, widening until it evens
out, into a small, round cave. Strewn among the rubble and gravel on the floor
are bones, pieces of skin and rotting pieces of flesh, no doubt the leftovers of
a predator.
name: Dank Den

desc:
The tunnel here is surrounded by a vast web, in which small, quivering balls are
embedded. The entirely of the web vibrates with the balls, as each step taken
sends the mass into roiling waves of white. A small, mossy hole opens in the
rock above, and the rocky tube extends eastward into the mountain.
name: Tunnel of Quivering Webs

desc:
The sticky mesh of white webs gets thicker as the tunnel deepens to the south.
Light and sound seem to be absorbed by it, as the web vibrates and quivers in
frequency to any disturbance in the air. A combination of scratching, and mushy
chewing, emanates from all directions.
name: Turn in a Sticky Tunnel

desc:
The web here is thicker, stickier, and hardier than that clinging elsewhere in
this muffled tunnel. The webs form large nets, containing bulks of drying
corpses, apparently the prepared dishes of unfortunate prey. Decaying corpses
lay in heaps on the floor, their identities erased by rot and time.
name: Tarantula's Lair

desc:
A natural stream of water gurgles up from deep within the limestone rock of the
cave system. The sound of trickling water is overwhelming as the rill flows
westward. A sizeable chute slopes steeply upwards, polished smooth by the
constant procession of water.
name: Cavern Spring

desc:
Rivulets of clear water seep from small cracks in the western cliff, forming a
large pool in the marshy ground below. Water-lilies provide cover for darting
minnows, and blue irises grow along the perimeter of the water. The land here is
soft and spongy, and travel easily deforms it.
name: Pond in the Northern Downs
